1: Thus says the LORD: Keep justice, and do righteousness, for soon my salvation will come, and my righteousness be revealed. The urgency of near salvation — the same eschatological pressure that frames every NT ethical exhortation. Romans 13:11-12: salvation is nearer to us now than when we first believed. The night is far gone; the day is at hand. 1 Peter 1:5: a salvation ready to be revealed in the last time. The salvation that is about to come is the revelation of Jesus Christ himself — the one Paul calls our righteousness (1 Corinthians 1:30). Isaiah 56:1 is the text whose urgency the entire NT inhabits: the kingdom is at hand, act accordingly.
2: Blessed is the man who does this, and the son of man who holds it fast, who keeps the sabbath, not profaning it, and keeps his hand from doing any evil. The beatitude-form connects to Jesus's Beatitudes (Matthew 5:3-12). Matthew 12:8: the Son of Man is lord of the Sabbath — Jesus claims sovereignty over the institution Isaiah 56:2 treats as the covenant marker. Hebrews 4:9-11: there remains a Sabbath rest for the people of God, for whoever has entered God's rest has also rested from his works as God did from his. Christ is both the Lord of the Sabbath and the Sabbath-rest itself — the one who fulfills the Sabbath's purpose so that his people cease their own works of merit and rest in his.
3: Let not the foreigner who has joined himself to the LORD say, The LORD will surely separate me from his people; and let not the eunuch say, Behold, I am a dry tree. The radical inclusion of those physically disqualified by the Mosaic code (Deuteronomy 23:1 excluded eunuchs from the assembly). Acts 8:26-40 narrates the direct fulfillment: Philip baptizes the Ethiopian eunuch on the road from Jerusalem — a physical eunuch, a foreigner, joining the covenant community through faith in Christ. The barrier that Isaiah 56:3 begins to dismantle is the barrier that the cross removes entirely: Galatians 3:28 — there is neither Jew nor Greek, slave nor free, male nor female, for you are all one in Christ Jesus.
4: For thus says the LORD: To the eunuchs who keep my sabbaths, who choose the things that please me and hold fast my covenant. The qualification shifts from physical status to covenant faithfulness — keeping sabbaths and holding fast the covenant, not biological wholeness. This is the logic of the new covenant: what matters is not circumcision or ethnic descent but new creation (Galatians 6:15). Philippians 3:3: we are the circumcision, who worship by the Spirit of God and glory in Christ Jesus and put no confidence in the flesh. The eunuch who holds the covenant is more fully in the assembly than the physically whole person who despises it.
5: I will give in my house and within my walls a monument and a name better than sons and daughters; I will give them an everlasting name that shall not be cut off. The everlasting name given in YHWH's house — better than biological legacy. Revelation 2:17: the overcomer receives a white stone with a new name written on it. Revelation 3:12: I will write on him the name of my God and the name of the city of my God... and my own new name. In Christ every believer receives an everlasting name inscribed by the risen Lord — an identity that death cannot cut off and infertility cannot diminish. The dry tree of verse 3 produces fruit that outlasts any family line.
6: And the foreigners who join themselves to the LORD, to minister to him, to love the name of the LORD, and to be his servants, everyone who keeps the sabbath and does not profane it, and holds fast my covenant. Full inclusion of Gentiles who love YHWH's name. Ephesians 2:12-13: those who were once alienated from the commonwealth of Israel and strangers to the covenants of promise, having no hope and without God in the world... have now been brought near by the blood of Christ. Romans 15:8-12: Christ became a servant to confirm the promises to the patriarchs so that the Gentiles might glorify God for his mercy — citing Isaiah 11:10 (the Root of Jesse as banner to the nations). Isaiah 56:6 is the OT ground of the Gentile mission.
7: These I will bring to my holy mountain, and make them joyful in my house of prayer; their burnt offerings and their sacrifices will be accepted on my altar; for my house shall be called a house of prayer for all peoples. Jesus quotes this verse directly at the temple cleansing: Mark 11:17 — Is it not written, My house shall be called a house of prayer for all the nations? But you have made it a den of robbers. Matthew 21:13; Luke 19:46 both record the citation. The temple action is Jesus's enacted commentary on Isaiah 56:7: the house of prayer for all peoples has been turned into an exclusionary marketplace. The court of the Gentiles — the only space where non-Jews could pray — had been commercialized. Christ restores the universal scope that Isaiah promised.
8: The Lord GOD, who gathers the outcasts of Israel, declares, I will gather yet others to him besides those already gathered. The promise of gathering beyond Israel — the inclusive scope of eschatological salvation. John 10:16: I have other sheep that are not of this fold. I must bring them also, and they will listen to my voice. So there will be one flock, one shepherd. John 11:51-52: Caiaphas unwittingly prophesies that Jesus would die to gather into one the children of God who are scattered abroad. The gathering of others beyond the gathered-Israel is the Gentile mission — and the Good Shepherd who gathers them is the fulfillment of the YHWH who gathers in Isaiah 56:8.
9: All you beasts of the field, come to devour — all you beasts in the forest. The summons of the devouring beasts shifts abruptly from the inclusive gathering (vv3-8) to predator-judgment language — setting up the indictment of faithless shepherds in vv10-12. Acts 20:29: Paul warns the Ephesian elders — after my departure fierce wolves will come in among you, not sparing the flock. Matthew 7:15: beware of false prophets, who come to you in sheep's clothing but inwardly are ravenous wolves. The beasts of the field that Isaiah summons are the false shepherds-turned-predators who devour what Christ gathers — the same contrast Jesus draws in John 10:10-12.
10: His watchmen are blind; they are all without knowledge; they are all silent dogs; they cannot bark, warning the people. The corrupt watchmen — blind, ignorant, silent where they should warn. Jesus's condemnation of the scribes and Pharisees in Matthew 23 covers this explicitly: you blind guides (Matthew 23:16, 24), blind fools (Isaiah 23:17), leaders who shut the kingdom in people's faces and do not enter themselves (Isaiah 23:13). The silent dogs who cannot bark are the religious leaders who see judgment approaching and give no real warning — absorbed in their own status rather than protecting the people. The contrast is the watchman-shepherd who lays down his life for the sheep (John 10:11).
11: The dogs have a mighty appetite; they never have enough. But they are shepherds who have no understanding; they have all turned to their own way, each to his own gain, one and all. The self-serving shepherds — Ezekiel 34:2-4 develops this theme (shepherds who feed themselves, not the flock). John 10:12-13: the hired hand, who is not the shepherd and does not own the sheep, sees the wolf coming and leaves the sheep and flees... he cares nothing for the sheep. The Good Shepherd who is the explicit contrast to this verse is Christ: I am the good shepherd. The good shepherd lays down his life for the sheep (John 10:11). Every self-serving religious leader is measured against the one who gave everything for those in his care.
12: Come, they say, let me get wine; let us fill ourselves with strong drink; and tomorrow will be like this day, great beyond measure. The corrupt leadership's hedonism — filling themselves with wine while judgment approaches. Luke 12:19-20: the rich fool says eat, drink, be merry and dies that night. Luke 12:45-46: the servant who says my master is delayed and begins to eat and drink and get drunk is cut in pieces when the master returns unexpectedly. Isaiah 56:1 opens with my salvation is near — the eschatological urgency that makes Isaiah 56:12's attitude criminal. The antithesis is the watching servant of Luke 12:35-40 who keeps his lamps burning and waits for the master's return.
